title: Mechanical Support in Myocardial Infarction Complicated by Cardiogenic Shock: What Have We Learned from Trials?
one_line_summary: AMI-CS is highly lethal. IABP offers no routine benefit. DanGer Shock showed Impella CP reduced 180-day **mortality** by 12.7% in selected STEMI-CS, despite complications. VA-ECMO trials showed no **survival** benefit. Individualized tMCS by shock teams is crucial.
key_pearls: Do not routinely implant temporary mechanical circulatory support (tMCS) in unselected AMI-CS patients.

For severe, progressive shock (SCAI Stage C or D) with intact neurological status and isolated LV failure, early Impella CP implantation (ideally pre-PCI) is **recommended** to optimize myocardial unloading and **survival**.

Reserve VA-ECMO strictly as rescue therapy for refractory biventricular failure, severe concomitant respiratory failure, or during active cardiac arrest (eCPR) in highly selected candidates with short low-flow times.
When VA-ECMO is deployed, actively monitor for LV distension and maintain a low threshold for implementing active LV unloading (e.g., ECMELLA protocol) to mitigate afterload-induced pulmonary edema.
Mandate the use of ultrasound-guided access, micropuncture techniques, and routine placement of a distal perfusion catheter to mitigate access-site complications.
Establish institutional "shock teams" comprising multidisciplinary specialists, as shock team-guided care is associated with higher 30-day and in-hospital **survival**.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology and Phenotypes of AMI-CS
sections.content: Cardiogenic shock complicates 5% to 10% of all AMIs. AMI-CS is not a uniform entity and can be classified into three distinct pathophysiological profiles: - Left Ventricular (LV)-Dominant Pump Failure ("Classic" Shock): Accounts for approximately 40% of cases. - Post-Cardiac Arrest Shock: Accounts for approximately 50% of cases, characterized by variable degrees of myocardial dysfunction combined with systemic ischemia-reperfusion injury and hypoxic brain injury. - Right Ventricular (RV) Failure or Mechanical Complications: Accounts for less than 10% of cases (e.g., ventricular septal defect [VSD], papillary muscle rupture). Immediate culprit-lesion percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) is a Class I, Level B recommendation. Routine immediate multivessel PCI is not **recommended** due to increased procedural risks, prolonged ischemic times, and contrast overload. Norepinephrine is preferred over dopamine as the first-line vasopressor due to a lower risk of tachyarrhythmias. Inotropes (dobutamine, milrinone, adrenaline) are added to augment cardiac output, though they increase myocardial oxygen consumption.

sections.heading: Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump (IABP)
sections.content: IABP works via diastolic counterpulsation, inflating during diastole to augment coronary perfusion and deflating immediately prior to systole to reduce aortic pressure and LV afterload. It provides modest hemodynamic support, typically increasing cardiac output by only 0.5 to **1.0 L**/min, with minimal direct effect on the RV. The IABP-SHOCK II trial randomized 600 patients with AMI-CS undergoing early PCI to IABP vs. standard medical care, showing no difference in 30-day **mortality** (39.7% IABP vs. 41.3% control, p = 0.69). Neutrality was sustained at 6-year follow-up, with comparable safety rates for major peripheral ischemia (4.3% vs. 3.4%) and life-threatening bleeding (3.3% vs. 4.4%). Guideline recommendations reflect this: - ESC 2023: Class III (No Benefit) for routine use; may be considered (Class IIb) in mechanical complications (VSD, papillary muscle rupture) as a bridge to surgery. - AHA/ACC 2025: Class III (No Benefit) for routine use; Class IIb for refractory shock when advanced tMCS is unavailable or for mechanical complications.

sections.heading: Microaxial Flow Pump (MAFP / Impella)
sections.content: Impella is a catheter-mounted microaxial pump placed across the aortic valve. It actively unloads the LV by drawing blood from the LV cavity and expelling it into the ascending aorta, providing continuous forward flow of 3.5 to **5.5 L**/min (depending on the model, e.g., Impella CP or Impella 5.5). This directly reduces LV end-diastolic pressure (LVEDP), volume, and myocardial oxygen demand while maintaining systemic perfusion. Clinical trial evidence includes: - IMPRESS Trial (n = 48): Evaluated severe STEMI-CS. A highly compromised cohort (~92% post-cardiac arrest, median lactate > **8 mmol**/L). Showed no difference in 30-day **mortality** (46% vs. 50%) or 5-year **mortality** (50% vs. 63%). The trial was underpowered and dominated by post-arrest hypoxic brain injury. - DanGer Shock Trial (n = 360): Randomized STEMI-CS patients to Impella CP plus standard care vs. standard care alone, crucially excluding comatose post-arrest patients. Impella CP was implanted very early (57% pre-PCI). Primary Outcome: **Significant** 12.7% **absolute** reduction in 180-day all-cause **mortality** (45.8% Impella vs. 58.5% control; HR 0.74, p = 0.04), corresponding to a Number Needed to Treat (NNT) of approximately 8. **Survival** benefit was sustained up to 10 years, with a lower NNT (~5) in patients younger than 77 years. Complications: Impella was associated with **significantly** higher rates of major bleeding (22% vs. 12%), limb ischemia (5.6% vs. 1.1%), renal replacement therapy (RRT; 42% vs. 27%), and sepsis (12% vs. 5%), yielding a Number Needed to Harm (NNH) of approximately 6 for serious device-related adverse events. Guideline recommendations: - ESC 2023: Class IIb (may be considered) in refractory shock at experienced, high-volume centers. - AHA/ACC 2025: Upgraded to Class IIa (reasonable to use to reduce **mortality**) in STEMI complicated by severe or refractory cardiogenic shock, reflecting the DanGer Shock data balanced against the complication risk.

sections.heading: Veno-Arterial Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation (VA-ECMO)
sections.content: VA-ECMO provides complete cardiopulmonary support by draining venous blood, oxygenating it, and returning it to the arterial system via retrograde flow. It provides high-flow support (3 to **5 L**/min) and supports biventricular failure. A major hemodynamic limitation is that retrograde arterial flow increases LV afterload, which can cause LV distension, pulmonary edema, and thrombus formation unless active LV unloading (venting via Impella [ECMELLA], surgical vent, or atrial septostomy) is performed. Clinical trial evidence includes: - ECLS-SHOCK Trial (n = 417): Randomized AMI-CS patients with planned early revascularization to early VA-ECMO vs. standard care. Primary Outcome: No difference in 30-day **mortality** (47.8% VA-ECMO vs. 49.0% control; RR 0.98, p = 0.81). Results remained neutral at 1 year. The cohort was sicker (over 60% SCAI Stage D/E; 78% post-cardiac arrest with median ROSC of 20 minutes). LV unloading was inconsistently applied. Complications: **Significantly** higher rates of major bleeding (23.4% vs. 9.6%) and vascular complications (11% vs. 4%) in the ECMO arm. - ECMO-CS Trial (n = 117): Compared immediate VA-ECMO to a selective, delayed rescue ECMO strategy in rapidly deteriorating shock. No difference was found in the composite primary endpoint (death, resuscitated cardiac arrest, or device crossover). - Extracorporeal CPR (eCPR): VA-ECMO deployed during active refractory cardiac arrest. Evidence is mixed: the single-center ARREST trial showed a **survival** benefit, whereas the multicenter INCEPTION trial showed no difference in 30-day **survival** with favorable neurological outcomes, highlighting that eCPR outcomes depend heavily on low-flow times and strict candidate selection. Guideline recommendations: - ESC 2023: Class III for routine use; Class IIb for refractory shock or cardiac arrest at expert centers with a plan for LV unloading. - AHA/ACC 2025: Class III (Not **Recommended**) for routine use in AMI-CS, reserving it strictly as rescue therapy.

sections.heading: Evidence Discrepancies and Real-World Registries
sections.content: The SCAI Shock Classification stratifies shock from Stage A (At Risk) to Stage E (Extremis). While useful, staging is dynamic, and treating any cardiac arrest as a Stage E modifier oversimplifies neurological risk. Real-world registry data (e.g., Dresden Impella Registry, Schrage et al.) show that only 24.4% of contemporary real-world AMI-CS patients would meet the strict inclusion/exclusion criteria of the DanGer Shock trial, and only 55.3% would meet IABP-SHOCK II criteria. Real-world patients are typically older, have more comorbidities, and present later in the shock course. In real-world registries, in-hospital **mortality** for patients treated with IABP or VA-ECMO often exceeds 50% to 60%, which is higher than the rates reported in clinical trials.

strengths_limitations: The DanGer Shock trial provides the first high-quality RCT evidence of a substantial 180-day **survival** benefit (12.7% **absolute** reduction) for Impella CP in a carefully selected, neurologically intact STEMI-CS population. However, high heterogeneity in trial populations (especially regarding post-cardiac arrest status and baseline lactate) limits cross-trial comparisons. The **survival** benefit of Impella CP in DanGer Shock is offset by a high complication rate (NNH of 6 for bleeding, limb ischemia, sepsis, or RRT), and there is a lack of large-scale head-to-head RCTs comparing different tMCS modalities.
